China's housing crisis is still getting worse:

New home prices in 70 Chinese cities fell -0.2% MoM in May, deteriorating from a -0.1% decline in April.

YoY, prices dropped -3.5% for the 2nd consecutive month.

Existing home prices fell -0.3% MoM, marking the largest monthly decline in 3 months.

China's property downturn has now weighed on the economy for nearly 5 years.

At its peak, the property sector accounted for ~25% of China's economy and it remains a major drag on growth.

China's 2008 moment is now for the housing sector.

BREAKING: The US Economic Surprise Index is up to 63.2 points, the highest since August 2023.

This index measures economic data relative to consensus estimates, turning positive when data beats estimates and negative when data misses.

This metric has risen +57.6 points since late April, posting the largest 7-week increase since Q1 2022.

The move comes amid stronger-than-expected jobs data, ISM Services PMI, factory orders, ADP employment, job openings, and ISM Manufacturing PMI.

The US Economic Surprise Index is now approaching the 79.6 peak recorded in July 2023, which would mark the highest level since the early 2021 pandemic recovery.

US economic data is crushing expectations.

You’re 100% right that I believed in LUNA five years ago and shilled it on the way up.

I also talked about the risks involved in early 2022 when I talked about how the leverage that worked well for it in a bull market would work against it in a bear market.

And I learned from that.

e.g. I thought a 90%+ haircut was possible when it was the hottest coin on the market - I never assumed it’d go to zero.

I learned that it’s important to take the time to challenge yourself and what the crowd says is a system that works and has zero chance of completely failing.

And I learned that I would way rather be an outspoken vocal advocate toward helping prevent that type of situation from happening again than I would be someone that doesn’t want to deal with the criticism of the crowd and sit on the sidelines and say nothing.

Wow, 3 limit ups in a row with WUS TW. Pretty sad I didn't take larger positions. 

WUS TW is 1 my 2 NAV arbitrage + independent growth trades. 

If you want context: WUS TW is a ~$1.12B PCB player with AI DC growth. 

Their stake in WUS Kunshan is $4.79B (11.4% of 42.04 billion)

So basically they're a $1.1B PCB player growing independently, sitting on ~$4.79B worth of another company that looks to IPOed on Hong Kong markets soon. 

And a successful activist investor in Palliser (with Ajinomoto and Toto), is pressuring them to trim some holdings along with a few others. 

Seems like a pretty insane disconnect, and I think there's a lot of potential here.

Deal or no deal? Once again, that's the market's question.

Here are my Sunday thoughts on #uranium, #gold, #silver, #copper, #fluorspar and #potash.

A week ago, investors were debating whether a US-Iran agreement would stabilize the region and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Today, the picture is far less clear. Iran has once again announced the closure of Hormuz, reminding markets that geopolitics can overwhelm diplomacy overnight.

I still believe some form of agreement is the most likely outcome. Iran needs oil revenues. The US wants lower energy prices, lower inflation, and eventually lower interest rates. The incentives remain powerful. But the road to a lasting deal may be far more complicated than many expected.

What continues to impress me most is uranium. Despite one of the most significant geopolitical disruptions in years, the long-term uranium price continues to trend higher. Utilities still face the same challenge: growing reactor demand and limited future mine supply.

Gold and silver have pulled back, but if tensions ease and oil prices move lower, I expect markets to begin pricing in future rate cuts again. A weaker USD, improving liquidity, and rising sovereign debt levels would be supportive for both metals.

Copper remains one of my highest-conviction long-term themes. AI infrastructure, data centers, electrification, grid expansion, and EV adoption all require enormous amounts of copper, while major discoveries remain scarce and permitting timelines continue to lengthen.

One mineral that deserves far more attention is fluorspar. It is essential for uranium conversion, steelmaking, aluminum production, semiconductors, batteries, and numerous chemical processes. As governments push to secure domestic critical mineral supply chains, fluorspar projects in stable jurisdictions could become increasingly strategic.

And it's not just energy security that matters. It's food security too. Disruptions in energy markets often ripple through fertilizer markets. Higher energy and transportation costs ultimately affect agricultural production. That's one reason I remain bullish on potash.

The world needs more energy. More electricity. More infrastructure. More critical minerals. And more food.

That's why I remain extremely bullish on uranium, copper, gold, silver, fluorspar, and potash over the coming years.

Uranium > $300, gold > $10,000, silver > $200, and substantially higher copper prices would not surprise me at all.

The long-term story remains intact.

Just my thoughts. Please do your own DD.

Follow me for regular market updates and visit my profile to see my top picks.

With #uranium approaching a structural supply crunch, #silver deficits persisting, #copper demand accelerating, and critical minerals becoming a strategic priority, here's where I currently see the most attractive risk/reward in the market.

My top picks across uranium, silver, copper, potash, fluorspar, and graphene 👇

These are not necessarily the safest investments. They are simply the companies where I currently see the most attractive risk/reward.

☢️ URANIUM

Producers & Developers
$CCJ – The blue-chip uranium name.
$KAP – Lowest-cost producer in the world.
$DNN – Fully permitted and advancing Wheeler River.
$NXE – One of the world's largest undeveloped uranium projects.
$UUUU – This week they received a conditional US government loan commitment of up to $725 million to expand domestic rare earth processing and strengthen critical mineral supply chains. Exposure to uranium, rare earths, and growing US strategic support.
$EU – Current U.S. production.
$UEC – Unhedged and ramping up production.
Explorers
$SYH – Drilling near Wheeler River and one of the best prospect generators in the Athabasca Basin.
$M.CN – Potentially the largest uranium deposit in the U.S. based on historic drilling and recent radiometric results. 655 Mlbs endowment in the area. Now included in URNJ, drill program about to start.
$NOBL – Recently published a maiden resource at Duck Creek with significant expansion potential.
$FUU – The team behind four discoveries. If JR and Tetra connect, this could become a monster.

🥈 SILVER
I own three established producers with great margins at current silver prices:
$HL
$AG
$PAAS
I also own these three explorers:
$HYMC – Drilling one of the largest precious metals systems in the world.
$GRSL.V – Recently reported a standout drill intercept.
$JTWO – My wildcard. Bonanza-grade silver samples suggest a much larger high-grade system.

🔌 COPPER
$CGNT – My primary copper exposure. A massive deposit with significant leverage to higher copper prices.

🌾 POTASH
$KCLI – Massive exploration target in Utah's Paradox Basin with district-scale potential.

💎 FLUORSPAR
$CTV.V – CleanTech Vanadium is moving from planning to execution. The company recently launched a major drill program at its Campbell-Crotser fluorspar project in Kentucky, targeting material for an initial underground mine plan and a planned 500 tpd processing operation.

🌐 GRAPHENE 
$HGRAF – Still my largest single holding. A unique graphene technology platform with potential applications across multiple industries.

Disclosure: I have paid partnerships with Myriad Uranium, Skyharbour Resources, J2 Metals, Noble Plains Uranium, American Critical Minerals, F3 Uranium and CleanTech Vanadium.

Not financial advice. Please do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
it’s said that to be a true science, then it must be testable and falsifiable. The scientific method (hypothesis->experimentation->theory) can then be used to discover truth within these testable domains

There must be a deep connection to AI’s mastery of verifiable truths. That is, there’s an AI-native version of the Scientific Method where if a system is highly verifiable (AlphaGo, Erdos problems, etc) then AI will eventually know everything. And there are domains that might be in-between (it may turn out lots of science is like this). We’re use embodied AI/robotics to move the bar over time. Then there be some areas which are inherently non-verifiable and/or irreducibly complex, which is the domain of taste and human essence

Just as falsifiability determines the line between science and “not science” it feels like verifiability becomes the line between “AI can do it” and “needs a human”

𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗜𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗹𝗽𝗵𝗮?

One of the most attractive hunting grounds for special situations is the $500 million to $3 billion market cap range.

Below $500 million, many companies still have unproven products, financing risks, or business models that may never work. Above a few billion dollars, tens of analysts, institutions, and algorithms are already competing to find every inefficiency.

Between $500 million and $3 billion sits a sweet spot. The companies are usually large enough to have a real product, real customers, and a proven business model, yet small enough that only a handful of specialists truly understand the story.

When sentiment turns negative, the market can dramatically misprice these businesses.

A few examples from the past 12 months:

Two of the stocks below doubled after my recommendation, one more than tripled, and one is currently up roughly 50%. The only one that has not doubled yet is $WGS, which I believe will eventually join the others.

$URGN traded around $11 before a negative FDA advisory committee vote sent the stock down to roughly $6.50.

The FDA later approved the product. In biotech, a successful approval often leads to a significant re-rating. What made this situation unusual was that the stock only moved to around $10 after approval.

That made little sense. Before the committee meeting and before the FDA approval, investors were already willing to value the company at roughly $11. The company had now received the approval that mattered most, yet the stock was trading below where it had traded before either event.

Immediately after the FDA approval, I wrote that the market reaction made very little sense(see tweet below). Here was a profitable company with an approved product and a strong competitive position, yet the market was valuing it below where it traded before receiving the approval. I published multiple articles explaining the disconnect and ultimately allocated roughly 10% of my own portfolio to the position.

The stock quickly moved toward $15 and eventually above $30.

$STAA collapsed after demand from China weakened and sentiment turned extremely negative. Yet the company still had a strong competitive position, a significant moat, and a product backed by decades of clinical data.

An acquisition proposal around $30.75 per share was rejected by some of the most knowledgeable shareholders in the sector, who believed the company was worth substantially more. Demand from China later recovered, sentiment reversed, and the stock more than doubled from the levels where I recommended it.

$ZIM traded around $15 when I recommended it. At the time, the company had roughly $22 per share in cash and a net asset value of around $60 per share. The highest price target among the six analysts covering the stock was $16, while the lowest was $6. Not a single analyst had a Buy rating.

Management was sandbagging expectations and doing little to highlight the company’s underlying value. Eventually, the market began to recognize the disconnect, and the stock more than doubled from my initial recommendation if you take into account dividends.

$WGS is my newest idea from this group. The stock fell roughly 50% after reimbursement changes despite owning one of the most valuable genomic datasets in the world. The market became fixated on a reimbursement issue while largely ignoring the long-term value of the business. The irony is that whole genome sequencing, which analyzes essentially the entire genome, was increasingly replacing whole exome sequencing, which examines only a small fraction of a patient’s DNA, yet genome testing was often reimbursed at a lower rate. In my view, the market focused on a short-term reimbursement distortion while overlooking the long-term value of the business and its data assets.  

The common theme is simple: these companies are large enough to matter but small enough to be misunderstood.

That is where some of the best alpha still exists.

tweets.text:
David Friedberg: It's not rich vs poor, it's makers vs takers.

@friedberg:

“ The great lie is that there are two sides to society, that is the rich and the poor.

And the great truth is that there are two sides that are the makers and the takers.

The lie is that the rich are unfairly rich and the poor are unfairly poor, and therefore, the poor must take from the rich.

But the truth is that it's the takers that tell you that lie, that the real truth is that artists, plumbers, electricians, woodworkers, computer scientists, people that build, people that make from all walks of life, all income levels, all wealth brackets, are the makers.

And the takers are what Sacks calls this intelligentsia, the analysts, the espousers, the armchair mechanics, the critics, the commentators, the politicians. They are the takers.

They are the people that watch the rest of society make stuff, build stuff, specifically doing things that create value for other people in society. That's what a maker is.”

Last week, we published a report titled China Consumer Stocks: The Most Asymmetrical Investment Opportunity.

While much of the investment community remains focused on tech and AI, we believe China's consumer sector now offers the most compelling risk-reward opportunity over the next 1–3 years. 

Our thesis is primarily built on four key pillars:

1. Domestic demand has fallen to levels last seen during the most severe pandemic lockdowns. 

2. Exports-led growth is likely to face three major headwinds. 

3. Chinese consumer stocks are approaching capitulation levels by every metric. 

4. The last time the consumer index reached comparable levels of pessimism in September 2024, the subsequent Politburo pivot triggered a 50% rally in just two weeks.

If you are interested in reading this report or learning more about our broader investment research, please reach out at: shanghaimacro@gmail.com.

tweets.text:
AUSTRALIA'S ENERGY MADNESS

I started this looking into why Australian households who installed rooftop solar are now being penalised for it. Victoria just slashed feed-in tariffs by 99 percent. The federal battery rebate pushes households to soak up their own excess at their own cost. The grid can't handle the midday surge because there is no flexible industrial load.

There is an obvious answer in plain sight. Bitcoin mining. Texas runs it through ERCOT. Miners curtail on signal and stabilise the grid in the process. Australia could be monetising solar oversupply at scale. Instead it taxes the households who installed the panels. And anyone who didnt or couldnt afford were taxed via higher interest rates due to govt spending waste. 

At the same time, the same governments pitch the data centre buildout as a growth driver. AEMO has 5.4 GW in the DC connection queue. That load is 24/7 baseload, landing on a grid exiting 5.7 GW of dispatchable coal by 2030 with no firm answer for the gap.

Look at the chart. Ten countries are wiring nuclear directly into AI infrastructure. US restarting Three Mile Island. UK's Sizewell C. UAE's Stargate backed by Barakah. Australia is the red bar. The only G20 advanced economy that has walked away from nuclear in the 2026-2030 sovereign AI window.

So: kneecap coal. Kneecap gas. Penalise the solar households Canberra paid to install panels. Refuse the one technology every serious peer is using to anchor sovereign AI. Then export the uranium that powers their data centres.

I'm no energy expert. But Australia needs some adults in the room.
